title: C string representation

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for C string representation; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: ISO C Working Group C23 status; ISO/IEC 9899:2024; provisions applicable to C string representation
sources.claim: A C string is a character sequence terminated by the first null character, stored within an array or other valid region large enough for content plus the terminator.

sources.locator: GNU C Language Introduction and Reference Manual; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to C string representation
sources.claim: A C string is a character sequence terminated by the first null character, stored within an array or other valid region large enough for content plus the terminator.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A C string is a character sequence terminated by the first null character, stored within an array or other valid region large enough for content plus the terminator.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: The array {'c','a','t','\0'} is a four-byte representation of the three-character C string cat. Your goal is to trace string length, storage capacity and termination in memory. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: while, do and for; Parameters and return values; One-dimensional arrays. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: A C string is a character sequence terminated by the first null character, stored within an array or other valid region large enough for content plus the terminator. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Character arrays need not contain a string, embedded nulls end string operations early, string literals are not modifiable, and encoding can use multibyte sequences. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Every char array is automatically null-terminated and safe to pass to strlen or printf with %s.

includes: Treat strings as character arrays terminated by a null character with finite capacity.
Trace string length, storage capacity and termination in memory.
excludes: Character arrays need not contain a string, embedded nulls end string operations early, string literals are not modifiable, and encoding can use multibyte sequences.

outcomes: Trace string length, storage capacity and termination in memory.

examples.title: C string representation: defined case
examples.situation: The array {'c','a','t','\0'} is a four-byte representation of the three-character C string cat.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: The array {'c','a','t','\0'} is a four-byte representation of the three-character C string cat.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Before using string functions, prove that a null terminator occurs within the valid array bounds and keep capacity for it.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Before using string functions, prove that a null terminator occurs within the valid array bounds and keep capacity for it.

examples.title: C string representation: boundary case
examples.situation: char label[3] = {'c','a','t'} is an array but not a C string because no null terminator fits.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: char label[3] = {'c','a','t'} is an array but not a C string because no null terminator fits.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Character arrays need not contain a string, embedded nulls end string operations early, string literals are not modifiable, and encoding can use multibyte sequences.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Every char array is automatically null-terminated and safe to pass to strlen or printf with %s.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Trace string length, storage capacity and termination in memory.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Every char array is automatically null-terminated and safe to pass to strlen or printf with %s.
misconceptions.correction: Before using string functions, prove that a null terminator occurs within the valid array bounds and keep capacity for it.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: Every char array is automatically null-terminated and safe to pass to strlen or printf with %s.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model C string representation: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
